name: Keukenhof & King's Day
tagline: Seven million hand-planted bulbs in bloom, then the whole country dresses in orange and takes the party to the canals.
dates: Keukenhof: 18 Mar – 9 May 2027 (official dates; peak tulip bloom mid-April). Koningsdag (King's Day): Tue 27 Apr 2027 — both fall inside the same trip window
history: The Keukenhof estate long predates its flowers. In the 15th century the grounds were the 'Keukenduyn' (kitchen dunes) of Countess Jacoba van Beieren, who hunted game and gathered herbs here for the kitchens of nearby Teylingen Castle — hence the name, literally 'kitchen garden'. A country house rose on the site in 1641 and grew into Keukenhof Castle, and in 1857 the landscape architects Jan David Zocher and his son Louis Paul — the same duo who later drew up Amsterdam's Vondelpark — reshaped the park in the English landscape style whose winding paths and mature trees still frame the flower beds today.

The modern spectacle was born in 1949, when a group of twenty leading Dutch bulb growers and exporters hit on the idea of using the estate as a permanent open-air showcase for the flower-bulb industry, the Netherlands' great export. The first exhibition opened in 1950 and was an instant sensation. Today some seven million bulbs — around 800 varieties of tulip alongside hyacinths, daffodils and orchids — are planted by hand each autumn, and roughly 1.4 million visitors from around the world pour through in an eight-week season, making 'the Garden of Europe' the most famous spring garden on earth.

King's Day is the other half of this Dutch spring double-bill. It began in 1885 as Prinsessedag for the young Princess Wilhelmina, became Koninginnedag (Queen's Day) on 30 April under Queen Juliana, and was renamed and moved to 27 April when Willem-Alexander became king in 2013. Its defining traditions are the vrijmarkt — the one day a year anyone may sell anything on the street without a permit, effectively the world's largest flea market — and the sea of orange worn for the House of Orange-Nassau. Amsterdam is its epicentre: the city's 850,000 residents are joined by up to a million visitors, and the canals become one continuous boat party.
whyGo: Nowhere else on earth concentrates this much colour in one place — striped tulip fields stretching to the horizon around Lisse, and a garden so meticulously planted it reads like a living painting. Time it right and you get two bucket-list experiences in one short trip: peak bloom at Keukenhof and, days later, the biggest street-and-canal party in Europe. In 2027 Keukenhof is still open on King's Day itself, so the combination genuinely works.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Amsterdam Schiphol (AMS), SWISS or KLM direct (several daily); then train Schiphol → Leiden Centraal (15 min) + Keukenhof Express bus 854/858 (~25 min)
gettingThere.notes: Book 2–3 months out; King's Day week fills up fast. Buy the combi ticket (bus + timed garden entry) via Keukenhof's site

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Amsterdam Centraal via Basel SBB and Frankfurt/Köln (ICE, 1–2 changes) — or the direct ÖBB Nightjet Zürich → Amsterdam overnight
gettingThere.cost: €60–160 one-way daytime; Nightjet from ~€70 seat / €120–180 couchette-sleeper
gettingThere.notes: The Nightjet lands you in Amsterdam at breakfast time with no hotel night lost — book 3–6 months ahead for cabins

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Basel → Frankfurt → Ruhr → Amsterdam (A3/A2), motorway nearly the whole way
gettingThere.cost: ~€120–180 in fuel + tolls/vignette each way, plus Dutch parking
gettingThere.notes: Only worth it for flexibility in the bulb fields; central Amsterdam is closed to cars on King's Day and parking is punitive — park at a P+R

costs.notes: Based on transport from Zürich + 3 nights + Keukenhof entry (€21.50 online). Accommodation is the swing factor: Amsterdam hotel rates double or triple around King's Day. Sleeping in Leiden or Haarlem instead cuts costs sharply; the splurge tier assumes a canal-side Amsterdam hotel and a private boat hire on 27 April

booking.label: Keukenhof tickets (timed entry, on sale mid-October)

weather: Dutch April is famously fickle: 8–16°C, bright spells trading places with sharp showers and a fresh North Sea wind. Pack layers, a waterproof shell, and comfortable shoes that can handle damp grass — and something orange for the 27th.
tips: Book Keukenhof timed-entry tickets online (€21.50 vs €25 at the gate) — the garden uses half-hour arrival slots and prime mid-April weekends sell out.
Arrive at 08:00 sharp or after 16:00 on a weekday; between 11:00 and 15:00, and on Easter weekend, the main axes get shoulder-to-shoulder.

Rent a bike at the Keukenhof entrance and loop the Bollenstreek flower fields around Lisse — the striped open fields are the photo most people actually come for. Never walk into the fields: they are farmers' crops.
Base yourself in Leiden or Haarlem rather than Amsterdam: rooms cost half as much in King's Day week, and both are 15–35 minutes from either event by train/bus.

For King's Day, go cashless-plus-cash (many vrijmarkt stalls are coins-only, bars are contactless), wear orange, and plan zero intra-city transport — the centre closes to cars and trams get rerouted; everything is on foot.
Don't skip Koningsnacht (26 April): the night before is Amsterdam's biggest club-and-street-party night, and it's the locals' half of the celebration.

localRecs.name: Café 't Smalle
localRecs.note: Canal-side brown café in the Jordaan with its own tiny jetty — arguably the single best King's Day vantage point in Amsterdam; arrive very early on the 27th

localRecs.name: Winkel 43
localRecs.note: On Noordermarkt in the Jordaan; the appeltaart widely considered the city's best — perfect fuel between vrijmarkt stalls

localRecs.name: Jordaan
localRecs.note: The King's Day epicentre: vrijmarkt streets, canal concerts and packed brown cafés — the classic way to experience the day

localRecs.name: Bollenstreek bike route (Lisse–Noordwijk)
localRecs.note: Waymarked cycle loop through the tulip, hyacinth and daffodil fields around Keukenhof — best colour mid-April, quietest before 10:00

localRecs.name: De Kas
localRecs.note: Restaurant inside a 1926 municipal greenhouse in Park Frankendael, cooking from its own gardens — the fitting splurge dinner for a flower-themed trip
